content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMIVIISSION
P.O. Box 807
. 428 J Street
. Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660
. Fax (916) 322-0886
March25, 1999
Sue McCloud
BoxM-1
Carmel, CA 93291
Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-99-070
Dear Ms. McCloud:
This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the honoraria provisions of
the Political Reform Act (the “Act”).’
QUESTION
As a city councilmember, may you accept a payment for your participation as a CIA
expert on a panel at a conference held by Yale University’s Whitney Humanities Center if your
participation is pursuant to the CIA’s outreach program for which you are a volunteer?
CONCLUSION
No. The payment is a prohibited honorarium.
FACTS
You were elected to the City Council of Carmel-by-the-Sea in April 1998. Prior to that
time, you spent 31 years as a senior officer of the Central Intelligence Agency (“CIA”) in
Washington, D.C. Afier your retirement in 1994, you became a volunteer for the CIA’s
“Outreach Program” run by the agency’s Public Affairs Office for which you receive no
remuneration. The outreach program involves former CIA employees making speeches about
CIA activities. As a volunteer, you receive regular unclassified mailings from the CIA, which
contain materials that you can use in your speeches, and you make speeches to various groups,
Government Code sections 81000
- 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109 -
18995, of the California Code of Regulations.


File No. A-99-070
Page 2
such as the World Affairs Council, Stanford University, rotary clubs, schools and so forth. This
month you have made two speeches and have more scheduled in the future. You regularly report
to the CIA the number of speeches you give. To date, you have not received or been offered any
honoraria.
Before you were elected to the city council, you were invited to speak at a conference,
“The Matter ofEspionage,” at Yale University’s Whitney Humanities Center, in Hartford,
Connecticut on February 26 and 27. The center has offered you $500 for your participation.
ANALYSIS
The Act prohibits an elected officer of a local governmental agency from accepting an
honorarium from any source. (Section 89502(a).) The term “honorarium” includes any payment
made in consideration for any speech given. (Section 89501(a).) A “speech” includes
participation on a panel. (Regulation 18931.1.)
After your retirement from the CIA in 1994, you became a volunteer for the CIA’s
Outreach Program. As a volunteer, you speak to various groups about the activities of the CIA.
You regularly report to the CIA the number of speeches you give. Before you were elected to the
city council, you were invited to participate on a panel at a conference held by Yale University’s,
Whitney Humanities Center. The center has offered you $500 for your participation.
The Act exempts from the definition of “honorarium” earned income for personal
services that are customarily provided in connection with the practice of a “bona fide business,
trade or profession.” (Section 89501(b)(1).) The exception has two elements. First, the services
must be provided in connection with an individual’s business or the individual’s practice or
employment in a bona fide business, trade or profession. The second element requires that the
services be customarily provided in connection with the business, trade or profession.
(Regulation 18932(a).)
The term “bona fide business, trade or profession” is defined in Regulations 18932.1-
18932.3. A business is presumed to be “bona fide” if the owner of the business maintains
specified documentation. (Regulation 18932.1(a) and (b).) An official is presumed to be
engaged in a “bona fide” trade or profession ifthe official possesses a current license or
certification. (Regulation 18932.1 (c)( 1).) Ifneither licensing or certification are required, an
official is presumed to be engaged in a bona fide trade or profession if the official is employed as
a researcher or is a faculty member at a college or university. (Regulation 18932.1(c)(2).)
Although you provide services as a volunteer for the CIA’s outreach program, the earned
income exemption does not apply to your facts. The CIA, a governmental entity, is not a bona
fide business, as that term is used for purposes of this exception. In addition, since you are a
volunteer, it does not appear that you have your own business. Moreover, as a former senior


File No. A-99-070
Page 3
officer for the CIA and present volunteer for its outreach program, you are not engaged in a bona
fide trade or profession as described above.
For your information, the term “honorarium” does not include a charitable donation made
under the following circumstances. The donation must be made directly to a bona fide tax-
exempt nonprofit organization. You may not make the donation a condition for your speech.
You may not claim the donation as a deduction for income tax purposes. The donation must not
have a financial effect on you or a member of your immediate family. Finally, you may not be
identified in connection with the donation. (Regulation 18932.5 (copy enclosed).) In addition,
the prohibition does not apply if you deliver the honorarium to the city’s general fund and you do
not claim the donation as a deduction for income tax purposes. (Regulation 18933(a)(2).)
The prohibition also does not cover free admission to and refreshments at an event in
which the official participates in a panel, nor does it apply to actual costs for transportation to the
event, including lodging and subsistence payments made the day immediately preceding, the day
of and the day immediately following the event. (Regulations 18932.4(e), (g) and 18950.1,
copies enclosed.) However, since the speech will be made outside the State of California, the
payments made for travel costs must be reported as gifts on your statement of economic interests.
Only intrastate travel in connection with a speech is exempt from reporting requirements.
(Regulations 18932.4(e); 18950.3.) Although reportable, these payments are not subject to the
$300 gift limit because they are provided in connection with a speech. (Regulation 18950.1(a).)
I have enclosed a fact sheet that provides additional information regarding gifts and travel.
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at
(916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel
By:
Julia Butcher
Staff Counsel, Legal Division
SGC:JB:tls
Enclosures

sections.question:
As a city councilmember, may you accept a payment for your participation as a CIA
expert on a panel at a conference held by Yale University’s Whitney Humanities Center if your
participation is pursuant to the CIA’s outreach program for which you are a volunteer?

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: As a city councilmember, may you accept a payment for your participation as a CIA
expert on a panel at a conference held by Yale University’s Whitney Humanities Center if your
participation is pursuant to the CIA’s outreach program for which you are a volunteer?

CONCLUSION: No. The payment is a prohibited honorarium.
